title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number

frames.caption: Each digit maps to 3-4 letters; we build one combination by picking one letter per digit. digits="23" -> 3 x 3 = 9 results in the order ad, ae, ... , cf.
frames.note: The decision tree has one level per digit and a branching factor of 3-4 (the letters on that key). Depth = number of digits, so for n digits with up to 4 letters there are at most 4^n leaves. We walk this tree depth-first: choose a letter, explore the next digit, un-choose. Total cost O(n * 4^n) — one copy per leaf.

frames.caption: index=0: digit 2 -> CHOOSE 'a'. path becomes "a". Explore Backtrack(1).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append one letter from THIS digit's 3-4 options, then EXPLORE the next digit by recursing with index+1. There is no start index and no used[] here — every position is independent, so this is a pure Cartesian product: each digit's letters cross with every choice of the digits that follow.

frames.caption: index=1: digit 3 -> CHOOSE 'd'. path becomes "ad". Explore Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append one letter from THIS digit's 3-4 options, then EXPLORE the next digit by recursing with index+1. There is no start index and no used[] here — every position is independent, so this is a pure Cartesian product: each digit's letters cross with every choice of the digits that follow.

frames.caption: index=2 == length: record combination "ad" (combo #1).
frames.note: BASE CASE: index has walked past the last digit, so the StringBuilder now holds one letter per digit — a complete combination. Record a COPY of the string (sb.ToString() snapshots it). Copying matters because sb keeps mutating as recursion unwinds; storing a live reference would leave every result aliasing the same buffer.

frames.caption: Back at index=1: UN-CHOOSE 'd' -> path is "a" again. Try the next letter of digit 3.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: drop the last character (sb.Length--) so the next sibling letter at THIS level starts from a clean prefix. This rewind is what makes it backtracking — without it, sibling branches would inherit each other's letters. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Done: 9 combinations = 3 x 3. Each of digit 2's 3 letters crosses with each of digit 3's 3 letters.
frames.note: There are exactly (letters of digit 1) x (letters of digit 2) x ... combinations — a Cartesian product, so no pruning can shrink it below the output size. Space (excluding output) is just O(n) for the StringBuilder and the recursion stack, since only one path is alive at a time.
